title: Using finished compost in the garden
subtitle: Mulching, digging in, potting mixes, and lawn top-dressing
excerpt: Finished compost improves soil structure, feeds soil biology, and suppresses weeds. The main application routes are surface mulching (no-dig), incorporation before planting, mixing into potting media, and lawn top-dressing. Each has a different application rate and timing.
sourceNotes: Charles Dowding no-dig gardening method. RHS guidance on compost application rates and timing. Garden Organic guidance on soil improvement with homemade compost.

glossaryTerms.definition: A growing method in which compost is laid on the soil surface rather than incorporated by digging. Worms and soil organisms pull the compost down into the soil profile. Avoids disturbing soil structure and weed seed bank; associated with Charles Dowding.

Applying a thin layer of compost or fine material over the surface of an established area, typically a lawn or perennial bed, without incorporating it. Material works down into the soil slowly. Application rate for lawns: 1-2 kg per square metre of sieved compost.

content.text: Finished compost is most valuable as a surface mulch applied in the

method: a 50-75 mm layer spread over the bed surface in autumn or early spring. Over the growing season, worms pull it down into the soil and the surface compost suppresses annual weeds. No cultivation is needed: simply plant or sow through the compost layer.
Surface mulch (no-dig beds): 50-75 mm depth applied once a year, autumn to early spring. Do not apply to frozen ground.
Incorporated into soil before planting: 2-4 kg per square metre dug in to a spade's depth. Most effective for hungry crops such as brassicas and courgettes.

Potting mix: blend sieved compost 1:1 with perlite, grit, or coir for potting on established plants. Homemade compost is not sterile and should not be used for seed sowing at high rates; blend at 20-30% with a commercial peat-free mix for seeds.
: sieve to 6 mm, apply 1-2 kg per square metre in early autumn. Brush into the grass surface with a stiff broom.
What finished compost does not replace

Homemade compost is a soil conditioner and gentle feed, not a concentrated fertiliser. Nitrogen content varies by feedstock but is typically 1-3% by dry weight, phosphorus 0.5-1%, potassium 1-2%. Heavy-feeding crops (sweetcorn, pumpkins, hungry brassicas) may need additional nitrogen from a more concentrated source (pelleted organic fertiliser or a nitrogen-rich mulch such as fresh grass clippings worked in). Compost alone on a very poor soil will improve structure over 2-3 seasons but will not achieve the same yield as a combined compost and fertiliser programme in year one.
